title: Testicular Cancer
oneLiner: Most common solid tumour in young men (15-45); highly curable if caught early; always consider in any scrotal lump.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Testicular torsion** — acute, severe, constant scrotal pain + nausea + vomiting + absent cremasteric reflex + high-riding testis. **The finding: "pain out of proportion to exam, no relief with elevation."** Do NOT delay urology referral for ultrasound if torsion suspected — time is testicle.
- **Fournier's gangrene** — scrotal pain with systemic toxicity (tachycardia, hypotension, confusion) + crepitus on palpation + dusky/necrotic skin. **The finding: "crepitus + shock."** Immediate surgery + broad-spectrum antibiotics — do not wait for imaging.
- **Ruptured testicular tumour with haemorrhage** — sudden severe pain + regional peritonism if retroperitoneal bleed. **The finding: "known or suspected testicular mass with acute hypotension."** Urgent CT + urology.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painless scrotal lump (most common — often noticed by partner)
  - Dull ache or heavy feeling in scrotum or lower abdomen
  - Acute scrotal pain (if haemorrhage, infarction, or torsion of tumour)
  - Gynaecomastia (due to hCG secretion, especially in non-seminoma)
  - Back or flank pain (retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy)
  - Unilateral testicular enlargement or firmness
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Firm, irregular, non-tender mass (unless acute complication)
  - Testis does not transilluminate (vs hydrocele)
  - Loss of testicular sensation (most useful sign — compare to contralateral side)
  - "Squishy" testis? No — tumour feels solid
  - Check for inguinal lymphadenopathy, abdominal mass, supraclavicular nodes (Virchow's)
  - Gynaecomastia or hirsutism (endocrine effects)
  - Atrophy of contralateral testis (risk of bilateral disease)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Epididymo-orchitis** — painful, tender, inflamed epididymis, fever, positive Prehn's sign (elevation relieves pain). **Splitter: no transillumination + loss of testicular sensation → tumour not infection.**
- **Hydrocele** — transilluminates, cystic, ballotable, cannot get above it. Rarely painful.
- **Varicocele** — "bag of worms" feel, disappears when supine, usually left-sided. Often asymptomatic.
- **Inguinal hernia** — cough impulse, can be reduced, above the scrotum, not attached to testis.
- **Testicular torsion** — acute onset, severe pain, absent cremasteric, high-riding testis. Age <20.
- **Spermatocele / epididymal cyst** — separate from testis, transilluminates, often painless.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Transillumination test (with penlight in dark room) — solid tumour does NOT transilluminate
  - Urinalysis — check for infection (pus cells, nitrites) to differentiate epididymitis
- **Bloods:**
  - Tumour markers (MUST send before orchidectomy): AFP (non-seminoma), hCG (seminoma and non-seminoma), LDH (bulk disease marker)
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, LDH (also tumour marker)
  - Reproductive hormones if infertility considered? Not routinely first-line.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler — differentiates intratesticular vs extratesticular; identifies vascularity, cystic vs solid. **Never-miss test: scrotal ultrasound for any testicular lump.**
  - **Second-line** (if cancer confirmed): CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast — stage retroperitoneal and distant metastases.
  - MRI scrotum if ultrasound equivocal (rare).
- **Special tests:**
  - Radical inguinal orchidectomy with histology — **never trans-scrotal biopsy** (risk of scrotal contamination and altered lymphatic drainage).
  - Sperm cryopreservation (sperm banking) before chemotherapy or radiotherapy.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Based on ultrasound features + elevated tumour markers + histology.
  - Staging: TNM (AJCC 8th edition) + IGCCCG prognostic classification for metastatic disease.
- **Never-miss test: Scrotal ultrasound** — omission delays diagnosis and worsens stage at presentation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected testicular cancer → urgent urology referral (same day if torsion ruled out)
  - If acute scrotal pain with suspicion of torsion → immediate urology + scrotal exploration (do not delay for imaging)
  - If Fournier's gangrene suspected → surgical review + IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin) + fluid resuscitation
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Pre-operative workup: tumour markers, CT staging (assess for metastases before surgery if possible), sperm banking discussion
  - Arrange radical inguinal orchidectomy (within 2 weeks of diagnosis)
  - If metastatic disease: start chemotherapy (BEP regimen) after histology and staging
  - **Default Orders (if admitted for surgery):** NBM from midnight, IV fluids (1L Hartmann's q8h), VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if CrCl >30), observations q4h
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Surveillance (CT + tumour markers every 3-6 months for 5 years, then annually)
  - Chemotherapy: BEP (Bleomycin, Etoposide, Cisplatin) x3-4 cycles depending on stage
  - Radiotherapy (for seminoma stage I or IIA/B)
  - Retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) for residual masses post-chemo
  - Hormonal replacement (testosterone) if bilateral orchidectomy
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Chemotherapy (first-line for metastatic non-seminoma):**
  - BEP regimen: Bleomycin 30,000 IU IV days 1,8,15 + Etoposide 100mg/m² IV days 1-5 + Cisplatin 20mg/m² IV days 1-5, repeated q21d. **Check local protocol** for exact doses and schedule.
- **Antiemetics (pre-chemotherapy):**
  - Aprepitant 125mg PO day 1, then 80mg days 2-3 + Ondansetron 8mg IV + Dexamethasone 8mg IV (per local policy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bleomycin → pulmonary toxicity (avoid high FiO2 during surgery; cumulative dose limit 400,000 IU)
  - Cisplatin → nephrotoxicity (ensure pre-hydration: 1L 0.9% saline over 1h before and after; monitor creatinine)
  - Etoposide → myelosuppression (check FBC before each cycle)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cisplatin: dose reduction if CrCl <60 mL/min; avoid if CrCl <30.
  - Bleomycin: no adjustment but caution in renal impairment (increases toxicity).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Cisplatin without adequate hydration** → acute kidney injury. Always pre-hydrate with 1L NS.
  - **Bleomycin in high FiO2** → acute respiratory distress syndrome. Document on anaesthetic chart: "Avoid high FiO2 — bleomycin lung risk."
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (rare; monitor urate, K+, phosphate)
  - Chemotherapy-induced neutropenic sepsis (fever >38°C + neutrophils <0.5) — emergency IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy)
  - Cisplatin nephrotoxicity (check creatinine before each dose)
  - Bleomycin pneumonitis (dry cough, dyspnoea, fever — stop bleomycin, consider steroids)
  - Surgical: haematoma, wound infection, scrotal haematoma
- **Chronic:**
  - Infertility (sperm banking critical before chemo)
  - Hypogonadism (testosterone replacement if bilateral orchidectomy)
  - Secondary malignancy (especially etoposide-associated leukaemia)
  - Cardiovascular disease (cisplatin increases long-term risk)
  - Raynaud's phenomenon (bleomycin-related)
sections.Prognosis: - Overall cure rate >95% (early stage). For metastatic disease, 5-year survival by IGCCCG risk: good (90%), intermediate (70-80%), poor (50-60%). Seminoma better than non-seminoma at all stages. Death mainly from metastatic progression or treatment toxicity.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine both testes** — 2-5% bilateral risk (synchronous or metachronous)
- **Offer sperm banking** before any chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or orchidectomy (if future fertility wanted)
- **Use two tumour marker panels** (AFP + hCG) — both negative does not exclude seminoma
- **Document testicular examination** clearly in notes — many complaints arise from missed lumps
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT perform trans-scrotal biopsy** or needle aspiration — contaminates scrotal lymphatics, alters management (will need scrotal radiation + node dissection)
- **Do NOT delay referral** for a painless lump — young men often reassured by "wait and see" (NICE recommends 2-week wait for scrotal lump)
- **Do NOT miss retroperitoneal metastases** — back pain in a young man with testicular lump requires CT, not plain films
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing scrotal pain, swelling, or redness
  - New back pain, abdominal pain, or leg swelling (DVT from retroperitoneal mass)
  - Fever >38°C, rigors, or feeling unwell (neutropenic sepsis if on chemo)
  - Shortness of breath (pulmonary embolus, bleomycin pneumonitis, or hydrothorax from metastases)
  - Inability to pass urine (mass effect)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urgent urology outpatient (within 2 weeks) if diagnosis not yet made
  - If diagnosed: oncology + urology joint clinic, tumour markers q2-3 months initially, CT per protocol
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (or any single parameter red) → call medical review within 30 minutes
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** or **pH <7.35** → consider sepsis, CT for metastatic disease, involve ICU
- **GCS <15** or **seizure** → brain metastases? Call oncology + CT head
- **Suspected neutropenic sepsis** (temp >38°C + chemo within 6 weeks) → start IV piperacillin-tazobactam within 1 hour, call oncology registrar
- **Acute scrotal pain with suspicion of torsion** → immediate urology on-call; do not wait for ultrasound
- **Fournier's gangrene** → senior surgeon + ICU; urgent surgical debridement

rawMarkdown:
## Testicular Cancer
**One-liner:** Most common solid tumour in young men (15-45); highly curable if caught early; always consider in any scrotal lump.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Testicular torsion** — acute, severe, constant scrotal pain + nausea + vomiting + absent cremasteric reflex + high-riding testis. **The finding: "pain out of proportion to exam, no relief with elevation."** Do NOT delay urology referral for ultrasound if torsion suspected — time is testicle.
- **Fournier's gangrene** — scrotal pain with systemic toxicity (tachycardia, hypotension, confusion) + crepitus on palpation + dusky/necrotic skin. **The finding: "crepitus + shock."** Immediate surgery + broad-spectrum antibiotics — do not wait for imaging.
- **Ruptured testicular tumour with haemorrhage** — sudden severe pain + regional peritonism if retroperitoneal bleed. **The finding: "known or suspected testicular mass with acute hypotension."** Urgent CT + urology.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Painless scrotal lump (most common — often noticed by partner)
  - Dull ache or heavy feeling in scrotum or lower abdomen
  - Acute scrotal pain (if haemorrhage, infarction, or torsion of tumour)
  - Gynaecomastia (due to hCG secretion, especially in non-seminoma)
  - Back or flank pain (retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy)
  - Unilateral testicular enlargement or firmness
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Firm, irregular, non-tender mass (unless acute complication)
  - Testis does not transilluminate (vs hydrocele)
  - Loss of testicular sensation (most useful sign — compare to contralateral side)
  - "Squishy" testis? No — tumour feels solid
  - Check for inguinal lymphadenopathy, abdominal mass, supraclavicular nodes (Virchow's)
  - Gynaecomastia or hirsutism (endocrine effects)
  - Atrophy of contralateral testis (risk of bilateral disease)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Epididymo-orchitis** — painful, tender, inflamed epididymis, fever, positive Prehn's sign (elevation relieves pain). **Splitter: no transillumination + loss of testicular sensation → tumour not infection.**
- **Hydrocele** — transilluminates, cystic, ballotable, cannot get above it. Rarely painful.
- **Varicocele** — "bag of worms" feel, disappears when supine, usually left-sided. Often asymptomatic.
- **Inguinal hernia** — cough impulse, can be reduced, above the scrotum, not attached to testis.
- **Testicular torsion** — acute onset, severe pain, absent cremasteric, high-riding testis. Age <20.
- **Spermatocele / epididymal cyst** — separate from testis, transilluminates, often painless.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Transillumination test (with penlight in dark room) — solid tumour does NOT transilluminate
  - Urinalysis — check for infection (pus cells, nitrites) to differentiate epididymitis
- **Bloods:**
  - Tumour markers (MUST send before orchidectomy): AFP (non-seminoma), hCG (seminoma and non-seminoma), LDH (bulk disease marker)
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, LDH (also tumour marker)
  - Reproductive hormones if infertility considered? Not routinely first-line.
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Scrotal ultrasound with Doppler — differentiates intratesticular vs extratesticular; identifies vascularity, cystic vs solid. **Never-miss test: scrotal ultrasound for any testicular lump.**
  - **Second-line** (if cancer confirmed): CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast — stage retroperitoneal and distant metastases.
  - MRI scrotum if ultrasound equivocal (rare).
- **Special tests:**
  - Radical inguinal orchidectomy with histology — **never trans-scrotal biopsy** (risk of scrotal contamination and altered lymphatic drainage).
  - Sperm cryopreservation (sperm banking) before chemotherapy or radiotherapy.
- **Diagnostic criteria:**
  - Based on ultrasound features + elevated tumour markers + histology.
  - Staging: TNM (AJCC 8th edition) + IGCCCG prognostic classification for metastatic disease.
- **Never-miss test: Scrotal ultrasound** — omission delays diagnosis and worsens stage at presentation.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If suspected testicular cancer → urgent urology referral (same day if torsion ruled out)
  - If acute scrotal pain with suspicion of torsion → immediate urology + scrotal exploration (do not delay for imaging)
  - If Fournier's gangrene suspected → surgical review + IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin) + fluid resuscitation
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Pre-operative workup: tumour markers, CT staging (assess for metastases before surgery if possible), sperm banking discussion
  - Arrange radical inguinal orchidectomy (within 2 weeks of diagnosis)
  - If metastatic disease: start chemotherapy (BEP regimen) after histology and staging
  - **Default Orders (if admitted for surgery):** NBM from midnight, IV fluids (1L Hartmann's q8h), VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40mg SC daily if CrCl >30), observations q4h
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Surveillance (CT + tumour markers every 3-6 months for 5 years, then annually)
  - Chemotherapy: BEP (Bleomycin, Etoposide, Cisplatin) x3-4 cycles depending on stage
  - Radiotherapy (for seminoma stage I or IIA/B)
  - Retroperitoneal lymph node dissection (RPLND) for residual masses post-chemo
  - Hormonal replacement (testosterone) if bilateral orchidectomy

### Drugs & Doses
- **Chemotherapy (first-line for metastatic non-seminoma):**
  - BEP regimen: Bleomycin 30,000 IU IV days 1,8,15 + Etoposide 100mg/m² IV days 1-5 + Cisplatin 20mg/m² IV days 1-5, repeated q21d. **Check local protocol** for exact doses and schedule.
- **Antiemetics (pre-chemotherapy):**
  - Aprepitant 125mg PO day 1, then 80mg days 2-3 + Ondansetron 8mg IV + Dexamethasone 8mg IV (per local policy)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Bleomycin → pulmonary toxicity (avoid high FiO2 during surgery; cumulative dose limit 400,000 IU)
  - Cisplatin → nephrotoxicity (ensure pre-hydration: 1L 0.9% saline over 1h before and after; monitor creatinine)
  - Etoposide → myelosuppression (check FBC before each cycle)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cisplatin: dose reduction if CrCl <60 mL/min; avoid if CrCl <30.
  - Bleomycin: no adjustment but caution in renal impairment (increases toxicity).
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Cisplatin without adequate hydration** → acute kidney injury. Always pre-hydrate with 1L NS.
  - **Bleomycin in high FiO2** → acute respiratory distress syndrome. Document on anaesthetic chart: "Avoid high FiO2 — bleomycin lung risk."

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (rare; monitor urate, K+, phosphate)
  - Chemotherapy-induced neutropenic sepsis (fever >38°C + neutrophils <0.5) — emergency IV antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam per local policy)
  - Cisplatin nephrotoxicity (check creatinine before each dose)
  - Bleomycin pneumonitis (dry cough, dyspnoea, fever — stop bleomycin, consider steroids)
  - Surgical: haematoma, wound infection, scrotal haematoma
- **Chronic:**
  - Infertility (sperm banking critical before chemo)
  - Hypogonadism (testosterone replacement if bilateral orchidectomy)
  - Secondary malignancy (especially etoposide-associated leukaemia)
  - Cardiovascular disease (cisplatin increases long-term risk)
  - Raynaud's phenomenon (bleomycin-related)

### Prognosis
- Overall cure rate >95% (early stage). For metastatic disease, 5-year survival by IGCCCG risk: good (90%), intermediate (70-80%), poor (50-60%). Seminoma better than non-seminoma at all stages. Death mainly from metastatic progression or treatment toxicity.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always examine both testes** — 2-5% bilateral risk (synchronous or metachronous)
- **Offer sperm banking** before any chemotherapy, radiotherapy, or orchidectomy (if future fertility wanted)
- **Use two tumour marker panels** (AFP + hCG) — both negative does not exclude seminoma
- **Document testicular examination** clearly in notes — many complaints arise from missed lumps

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT perform trans-scrotal biopsy** or needle aspiration — contaminates scrotal lymphatics, alters management (will need scrotal radiation + node dissection)
- **Do NOT delay referral** for a painless lump — young men often reassured by "wait and see" (NICE recommends 2-week wait for scrotal lump)
- **Do NOT miss retroperitoneal metastases** — back pain in a young man with testicular lump requires CT, not plain films

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Increasing scrotal pain, swelling, or redness
  - New back pain, abdominal pain, or leg swelling (DVT from retroperitoneal mass)
  - Fever >38°C, rigors, or feeling unwell (neutropenic sepsis if on chemo)
  - Shortness of breath (pulmonary embolus, bleomycin pneumonitis, or hydrothorax from metastases)
  - Inability to pass urine (mass effect)
- **Follow-up:**
  - Urgent urology outpatient (within 2 weeks) if diagnosis not yet made
  - If diagnosed: oncology + urology joint clinic, tumour markers q2-3 months initially, CT per protocol

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** (or any single parameter red) → call medical review within 30 minutes
- **Lactate >2 mmol/L** or **pH <7.35** → consider sepsis, CT for metastatic disease, involve ICU
- **GCS <15** or **seizure** → brain metastases? Call oncology + CT head
- **Suspected neutropenic sepsis** (temp >38°C + chemo within 6 weeks) → start IV piperacillin-tazobactam within 1 hour, call oncology registrar
- **Acute scrotal pain with suspicion of torsion** → immediate urology on-call; do not wait for ultrasound
- **Fournier's gangrene** → senior surgeon + ICU; urgent surgical debridement
